So the, like we break down the article generation process into multiple steps.
So we start with the input, which is the, we provide the title, like the topic.
And then we give.
an outline that we research kind of semi manually.
So we research like the target keyword.
We take a look at like, what is the top ranking content?
What's the structure of that content?
Like, just to make sure that we're providing all the information that Google deems like valuable for a specific keyword.
And then we work, you know, we use AI to kind of synthesize that information and generate an outline that we plug into the assignment direction.
So it's a very, we spend a lot of time developing the outline because if the outline is good, like we'll spend less time editing the draft.
And it's very important also for AI search optimization that the structure of the content flows in a logical way, that each section is unique and separate from the other sections in the article.
I don't know if you've ever tried to write.

An article with AI, but if you just give it a title and ask it, okay, come up with an outline for this, you know, article, sometimes it will generate sections that there's quite a bit of overlap between them.
And from just like a writing quality standpoint, it's not good, but it's also bad for LLMs because they use chunking to extract information.
And if we don't follow those practices, then it's going to be harder for the LLMs to, like, understand the information and then cite it back to users.
So once we're happy with an outline, we proceed with the researcher.
The researcher step uses Tavali.
Tavali is a search API, which is pretty powerful.
It's able to find really high quality information and we can restrict the sources that it's accessing for...
You know, a specific client.
So we might have clients that are in healthcare, and they only want us to, you know, cite resources with a specific, like, domain, like .gov or .org.

And the researcher is going to take a look at our draft, and it's going to ask questions about the draft that it needs to answer in order to provide, create a very comprehensive research document that the AI is going to reference during the article generation process.
So this allows us to just write very comprehensive articles, because the AI has this very comprehensive research document with high-quality information.
I don't know, like, you might not know Tavali, but we used to use Perplexity, for example.
And Perplexity is not as powerful as Tavali in terms of research quality.
And then the researcher is going to evaluate itself.
So once it answers all of the questions that it needed to answer about the article, topic, it's going to go back and analyze if the information that it found actually answered those questions.
And if it didn't answer those questions, it's going to go back and try to fill the gaps of information.
And if it cannot find those gaps, then it's going to be reflected in the content.
So it's not going to use incomplete information or outdated information for the final draft of the article.
And then after that step is complete, we draft the article.
We add internal links.
After this step, we also add source links, which is a step where we, where the AI...
is going to add external links to every claim that it's making.
So for example, if we're referencing NIST or PCI compliance, it's going to add like source links to those websites or any like third party stats.
And then after that step, it validates the writing guidelines.
So the writing guidelines artifact is pulled in, it's called at the drafting stage, but AI sometimes has trouble following instructions, like all of the instructions that we provided.
So sometimes it might ignore some aspects of the writing guidelines because they might be contradicting or just because it's AI.
And sometimes it might decide to listen to specific instructions and ignore others.
So by adding this extra validation step, it basically, what it does is it takes the article draft and then it grades the draft.

Against the writing guidelines and the proofreading checklist, and if it identifies any paragraphs or any sentences that don't follow the writing guidelines or the proofreading checklist, it's going to go back and fix that.
So it just means less manual editing on our end.
And then we generate the meta tags, we run a fact-checking step, and then we will review and edit the final draft of the article before we send it to you.
